final_message:
Hello there! I'm Pal, your friendly math tutor, and I am so excited to learn with you today! Today we are going to explore something magical called reflection symmetry. Imagine taking a piece of paper with a shape on it and folding it right down the middle. That fold line is called a line of symmetry. If a shape has reflection symmetry, the two halves will be perfect mirror images of each other! 

Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry, which means the left and right sides match perfectly, just like the letter A. Other shapes have a horizontal line where the top and bottom match, and special letters like H actually have both! You can even make your own symmetric shapes by drawing half a picture on folded paper, cutting it out, and unfolding it. Have you ever tried cutting out a paper heart like that? 🌟

orchestrator_intent.goal: introduce the concept of reflection symmetry
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The section has not started yet, so the dialogue agent should introduce the new concept.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.

student_message: Yes! I made a pink paper heart for my mom.
final_message: A pink paper heart is so beautiful! Your mom is going to love it. Did you know your paper heart is a perfect example of reflection symmetry? If you fold your heart straight down the middle, the left side and the right side will match up perfectly. They are perfect mirror images of each other! That fold line right down the middle is called a line of symmetry. Because the line goes straight up and down, we call it a vertical line of symmetry. Can you try folding your pink heart in half right down the middle to see if the two sides match perfectly? 💖

student_message: A ladybug! Fold it straight down the middle.
final_message: Spot on! A ladybug folded straight down the middle is a perfect example. Now, let's look at a brand new kind of symmetry together called rotational symmetry. A shape has rotational symmetry if it looks exactly the same after being rotated, or turned, around its center point by just a fraction of a full turn, like a $$1/4$$ turn or a $$1/2$$ turn. If it only looks the same after a complete spin, it doesn't count! You can even draw a little dot on the shape to track where it goes as you turn it. Did you know a starfish is a living math puzzle? ⭐ Because of rotational symmetry, if you spin a starfish, it looks exactly the same five different times before making a full circle! What other cool everyday object can you think of that spins and looks perfectly the same? 🤔

orchestrator_intent.goal: Introduce rotational symmetry and re-engage visual attention.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The section is not_started, so the primary agent must introduce the new concept. The student is happy but has been looking away for 7 consecutive turns, so the engagement agent is needed to guide her visual focus back to the lesson.

current_section_content: A shape has rotational symmetry if it looks exactly the same after being rotated (turned) by a fraction of a full turn around its centre point. The fractions we check are 1/4 turn, 1/2 turn, and 3/4 turn. A shape that only looks the same after a full turn does not count as having rotational symmetry. You can track this by placing a dot on the shape and observing where the dot ends up after each turn.

current_section_content: You can deliberately create designs that have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or both by carefully arranging or colouring shapes. A design has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a fractional turn, and it has reflection symmetry if a mirror line splits it into two matching halves. The same set of shapes — such as squares and equilateral triangles with equal side lengths — can be arranged in different ways to produce designs with different symmetry properties. Designs can be sorted into three groups: only rotational symmetry, only reflection symmetry, or both.

current_section_content: You can deliberately create designs that have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or both by carefully arranging or colouring shapes. A design has rotational symmetry if it looks the same after a fractional turn, and it has reflection symmetry if a mirror line splits it into two matching halves. The same set of shapes — such as squares and equilateral triangles with equal side lengths — can be arranged in different ways to produce designs with different symmetry properties. Designs can be sorted into three groups: only rotational symmetry, only reflection symmetry, or both.
session_summary: The session covered lines of symmetry and rotational symmetry. The student understood these well, successfully identifying vertical and rotational symmetry in everyday objects and letters (O, X). However, the student struggled significantly with focus, frequently interrupting with off-topic subjects like dogs, a lost tooth, and imaginary creatures. The student's current learning state reflects a solid grasp of symmetry concepts, but their severe distractibility means they require constant redirection to stay on task.

final_message:

Speaking of fun things, let's look at a special art called block printing! In Rajasthan, artists carve beautiful designs into wooden blocks, dip them in ink, and stamp them onto fabric to make repeating patterns. Because the block is pressed face-down, the print on the fabric is actually a perfect mirror image of the carved block. 

I have a fun riddle for you about this! I dive into ink and stamp on the page. If you fold my picture in half, both sides match perfectly, just like a beautiful butterfly's wings. I help make the coolest, most balanced block prints. Can you guess what kind of mirror magic I am? ✨

orchestrator_intent.goal: Introduce symmetry in block printing patterns and re-engage student.

current_section_content: Block printing is a traditional craft from Rajasthan where artisans carve a design into a wooden block and press it repeatedly onto fabric to create beautiful repeating patterns. When the same block is stamped multiple times in different orientations, the final printed design can have rotational symmetry, reflection symmetry, or both. Importantly, the print produced by a block is the mirror image of the carved block, because the block is pressed face-down. By analysing how a block is repeated and rotated, you can predict and identify the symmetry of the resulting pattern.
